answer: {"question_type":"comparison","title":"Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat ARQ","summary":"Both are pipelined ARQ protocols, but Selective Repeat retransmits less and needs more buffering.","main_explanation":"Worked example with window size 4: sender transmits packets 1, 2, 3, 4, and packet 2 is lost. In **Go-Back-N**, the receiver discards out-of-order packets 3 and 4, so the sender must resend 2, 3, and 4. In **Selective Repeat**, the receiver keeps 3 and 4, ACKs them individually, and the sender resends only packet 2. So SR is more efficient after loss, but it needs more complex sender/receiver logic and buffering.","table":{"headers":["Aspect","Go-Back-N (GBN)","Selective Repeat (SR)"],"rows":[["Receiver behavior on out-of-order packet","Discards it","Buffers it"],["ACK style","Usually cumulative ACK","Individual ACKs for packets"],["After packet 2 is lost","Resend 2, 3, 4, ... from the missing one onward","Resend only packet 2"],["Buffer requirement","Small receiver buffer","Larger receiver buffer"],["Complexity","Simpler","More complex"],["Efficiency under loss","Lower","Higher"]]},"diagram":null,"elec3120_context":"In the transport-layer reliability discussion, the course emphasizes that both protocols allow many packets in flight, but Selective Repeat is more efficient at recovering from loss.","exam_tip":"If asked for the key difference, state: GBN discards out-of-order packets and retransmits a whole suffix; SR buffers out-of-order packets and retransmits only the missing ones.","check_understanding":"If packets 5, 6, 7 arrive after packet 4 is lost, what does the receiver do in GBN and in SR?","calculation_steps":{"setup":"Assume window size $4$. Sender sends packets $1,2,3,4$, packet $2$ is lost, and packets $3$ and $4$ still reach the receiver. Find how many packets must be retransmitted in GBN and SR.","steps":["Initial transmission: sender sends $4$ packets: $1,2,3,4$.","Loss event: packet $2$ is missing at the receiver.","GBN receiver action: packets $3$ and $4$ are out of order, so they are discarded.","GBN retransmission: sender must resend from the missing packet onward, so it resends $2,3,4$ -> $3$ packets.","SR receiver action: packets $3$ and $4$ are buffered and ACKed individually.","SR retransmission: sender resends only the missing packet $2$ -> $1$ packet."],"answer":"For this example, Go-Back-N retransmits 3 packets ($2,3,4$), while Selective Repeat retransmits only 1 packet ($2$).","common_mistakes":"A common mistake is thinking SR also discards out-of-order packets; actually SR keeps them and waits only for the missing one."},"response_style":"full_explanation"}

retrieved_materials.content:
• Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat both handle loss, while allowing lots 
of packets in flight.
• Selective repeat is more efficient at recovering from failure.
Handling Loss

retrieved_materials.content:
3.4  •  PRINCIPLES OF RELIABLE DATA TRANSFER     221
if every time a word was garbled, the surrounding 1,000 words (for example, a win-
dow size of 1,000 words) had to be repeated. The dictation would be slowed by all 
of the reiterated words.
As the name suggests, selective-repeat protocols avoid unnecessary retrans-
missions by having the sender retransmit only those packets that it suspects were 
received in error (that is, were lost or corrupted) at the receiver. This individual, 
as-needed, retransmission will require that the receiver individually acknowledge 
correctly received packets. A window size of N will again be used to limit the num-
ber of outstanding, unacknowledged packets in the pipeline. However, unlike GBN, 
the sender will have already received ACKs for some of the packets in the window. 
Figure 3.23 shows the SR sender’s view of the sequence number space. Figure 3.24 
details the various actions taken by the SR sender.
The SR receiver will acknowledge a correctly

retrieved_materials.content:
246     CHAPTER 3  •  TRANSPORT LAYER
Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat?
Let us close our study of TCP’s error-recovery mechanism by considering the fol-
lowing question: Is TCP a GBN or an SR protocol? Recall that TCP acknowledg-
ments are cumulative and correctly received but out-of-order segments are not 
individually ACKed by the receiver. Consequently, as shown in Figure 3.33 (see 
also Figure 3.19), the TCP sender need only maintain the smallest sequence number 
of a transmitted but unacknowledged byte (SendBase) and the sequence number 
of the next byte to be sent (NextSeqNum). In this sense, TCP looks a lot like a 
GBN-style protocol. But there are some striking differences between TCP and Go-
Back-N. Many TCP implementations will buffer correctly received but out-of-order 
segments [Stevens 1994]. Consider also what happens when the sender sends a 
sequence of segments 1, 2, . . . , N, and all of the segments arrive in order without 
error at the receiver. Further suppose that the

retrieved_materials.content:
22 shows the operation of the GBN protocol for the case of a window 
size of four packets. Because of this window size limitation, the sender sends pack-
ets 0 through 3 but then must wait for one or more of these packets to be acknowl-
edged before proceeding. As each successive ACK (for example, ACK0 and ACK1) 
is received, the window slides forward and the sender can transmit one new packet 
(pkt4 and pkt5, respectively). On the receiver side, packet 2 is lost and thus packets 
3, 4, and 5 are found to be out of order and are discarded.
Before closing our discussion of GBN, it is worth noting that an implementa-
tion of this protocol in a protocol stack would likely have a structure similar to that 
of the extended FSM in Figure 3.20. The implementation would also likely be in 
the form of various procedures that implement the actions to be taken in response to 
the various events that can occur. In such event-based programming, the various 
procedures are called (invoked) either by
